packages.name: prisma
packages.synopsis: Package prisma is a small, type-safe query builder and lightweight ORM over the standard database/sql package, inspired by the ergonomics of Prisma.
packages.doc:
Package prisma is a small, type-safe query builder and lightweight ORM over
the standard database/sql package, inspired by the ergonomics of Prisma. It
has no dependencies beyond the Go standard library.

# Overview

There are three layers:

  - The model layer compiles a Go struct into a [Model] using reflection and
    struct tags. A [Registry] stores compiled models and resolves them by
    type or name.
  - The builder layer is a generic, chainable [Query] that produces
    parameterized SQL. Literal values are always bound as arguments and never
    concatenated into the SQL text.
  - The runtime layer executes queries against a *sql.DB through a [Client]
    and scans result rows back into structs.

# Defining a model

Models are ordinary structs annotated with the "prisma" struct tag:

	type User struct {
	    ID    int64  `prisma:"col=id,pk,auto"`
	    Name  string `prisma:"col=name"`
	    Email string `prisma:"col=email"`
	    Posts []Post `prisma:"relation,fk=author_id,references=id"`
	}

	type Post struct {
	    ID       int64  `prisma:"col=id,pk,auto"`
	    Title    string `prisma:"col=title"`
	    AuthorID int64  `prisma:"col=author_id"`
	    Author   *User  `prisma:"relation,fk=author_id,references=id"`
	}

Recognized tag options are documented on [Registry.Register]. Column and table
names default to snake_case (tables are also pluralized) unless overridden by
a tag or a [TableNamer] implementation.

# Building queries

Create a [Client], register the models, then start a query with the generic
[NewQuery] constructor:

	client := prisma.NewClient(db)
	client.Register(User{})

	q := prisma.NewQuery[User](client).
	    Where(prisma.Equals("email", "a@b.com"), prisma.Gt("id", 10)).
	    OrderBy("name", prisma.Asc).
	    Take(20)

Predicates are built with the operator helpers ([Equals], [Not], [In], [Lt],
[Lte], [Gt], [Gte], [Contains], [StartsWith], [EndsWith]) and combined with
[And], [Or] and [NotGroup].

# SQL generation

Every terminal operation has a matching *SQL method that returns the exact
statement and argument slice without touching the database, which makes the
output easy to assert in tests:

	sqlStr, args, _ := q.FindManySQL()
	// SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE email = ? AND id > ?
	//   ORDER BY name ASC LIMIT ?
	// args: ["a@b.com", 10, 20]

The available terminal operations are [Query.FindMany], [Query.FindFirst],
[Query.FindUnique], [Query.Count], [Query.Create], [Query.CreateMany],
[Query.Update], [Query.Delete] and [Query.Upsert].

# Beyond basic CRUD

The builder also supports:

  - Aggregations via [Query.Aggregate] and grouped aggregation via
    [Query.GroupBy] (with HAVING).
  - Relation filters [Some], [Every], [None] and [Is], compiling to
    correlated EXISTS subqueries that compose inside [And]/[Or].
  - [Query.Distinct], keyset [Query.Cursor] pagination and NULLS-aware
    [Query.OrderByNulls] ordering.
  - Nested writes through [Query.NestedCreate] (create / connect /
    connectOrCreate / update on to-many relations).
  - Interactive and batched transactions via [Client.Transaction] and
    [Client.Batch], with queries started by [NewTxQuery].
  - Raw SQL via [Client.QueryRaw], [QueryRawInto] and [Client.ExecuteRaw].
  - JSON path filters [JSONPath] / [JSONEquals].
  - Typed error codes ([Error]) such as P2002 (unique) and P2025 (not found).

# Execution

The executing methods run the generated SQL against the client's *sql.DB and,
for reads, scan each row into a struct (including a to-one relation loaded via
[Query.Include]). Placeholder style is controlled by the [Dialect]: [Question]
("?") is the default and [Dollar] ("$1", "$2", ...) targets PostgreSQL.

consts.doc:
Prisma-style error codes surfaced by [Error.Code]. Only the subset that this
driver-agnostic library can reliably detect from database/sql errors is
mapped; the codes mirror Prisma's client error reference so callers familiar
with Prisma can branch on the same values.

vars.doc: Sentinel errors for matching a mapped [Error] by code with [errors.Is].

vars.signature:
var Dollar = Dialect{
	Name:        "dollar",
	Placeholder: func(n int) string { return "$" + strconv.Itoa(n) },
	DistinctOn:  true,
	NativeNulls: true,
	JSONExtract: jsonExtractPostgres,
	Upsert:      OnConflict,
}
vars.doc:
Dollar uses "$1", "$2", ... placeholders (PostgreSQL, pq/pgx). It supports
DISTINCT ON, native NULLS FIRST/LAST ordering and #>> JSON extraction.

vars.signature: var ErrNotFound = errors.New("prisma: record not found")
vars.doc:
ErrNotFound is returned by FindUnique and FindFirst when no row matches. It
wraps the P2025 "record not found" code (see [Error]).

vars.signature:
var MySQLDialect = Dialect{
	Name:        "mysql",
	Placeholder: question,
	DistinctOn:  false,
	NativeNulls: false,
	JSONExtract: jsonExtractSQLite,
	Upsert:      OnDuplicateKey,
}
vars.doc:
MySQLDialect uses "?" placeholders with MySQL's "ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE"
upsert syntax and "INSERT IGNORE" for skip-duplicate inserts.

vars.signature:
var Question = Dialect{
	Name:        "question",
	Placeholder: question,
	DistinctOn:  false,
	NativeNulls: false,
	JSONExtract: jsonExtractSQLite,
	Upsert:      OnConflict,
}
vars.doc:
Question is the default dialect using "?" placeholders (MySQL, SQLite, and
the standard database/sql convention for many drivers). It uses ON CONFLICT
upsert syntax (SQLite-compatible) and json_extract for JSON paths.

types.name: AggregateQuery
types.doc:
AggregateQuery builds an aggregation over the rows selected by a base
[Query]. Chain [AggregateQuery.Count], [AggregateQuery.Sum],
[AggregateQuery.Avg], [AggregateQuery.Min] and [AggregateQuery.Max], then call
[AggregateQuery.Exec] (or [AggregateQuery.SQL] to inspect the statement).

methods.name: Avg
methods.doc: Avg adds AVG(column) for each column.

methods.name: Count
methods.doc: Count adds COUNT(*) to the aggregation.

methods.name: Exec
methods.doc: Exec runs the aggregation and returns the scalar results.

methods.name: Max
methods.doc: Max adds MAX(column) for each column.

methods.name: Min
methods.doc: Min adds MIN(column) for each column.

methods.name: SQL
methods.doc:
SQL returns the aggregate SELECT statement and bound arguments without
executing it.

methods.name: Sum
methods.doc: Sum adds SUM(column) for each column.

types.name: AggregateResult
types.doc:
AggregateResult holds the scalar results of an [AggregateQuery]. Count is the
row count (present only when Count was requested). The maps are keyed by
column name and present only for the functions that were requested.

types.name: BatchFunc
types.signature: type BatchFunc func(ctx context.Context, tx *Tx) (int64, error)
types.doc:
BatchFunc is a single operation in a [Client.Batch]. It receives the shared
transaction and returns a per-operation result (typically a rows-affected
count) or an error that aborts the whole batch.

types.name: Client
types.doc:
Client is the entry point for building and executing queries. It bundles a
*sql.DB, a model Registry and a Dialect. A Client with a nil DB can still
build SQL (via the *SQL methods) but cannot execute.

funcs.name: NewClient
funcs.doc: NewClient constructs a Client around db (which may be nil for SQL-only use).

methods.name: Batch
methods.doc:
Batch runs ops sequentially inside one transaction and returns each op's
result. If any op returns an error the transaction is rolled back and no
results are returned. This mirrors Prisma's array form of $transaction, where
a list of independent writes commit or roll back together.

methods.name: DB
methods.doc: DB returns the underlying *sql.DB (may be nil).

methods.name: ExecuteRaw
methods.signature: func (c *Client) ExecuteRaw(ctx context.Context, query string, args ...any) (int64, error)
methods.doc:
ExecuteRaw runs an arbitrary non-query statement (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DDL)
with positional parameter binding and returns the number of rows affected.
This is Prisma's $executeRaw.

methods.name: QueryRaw
methods.signature: func (c *Client) QueryRaw(ctx context.Context, query string, args ...any) ([]map[string]any, error)
methods.doc:
QueryRaw runs an arbitrary SQL query with positional parameter binding and
returns each row as a map from column name to value. Placeholders in the SQL
must match the client's [Dialect]. This is Prisma's $queryRaw with dynamic
(map) results.

methods.name: Register
methods.doc: Register compiles and stores the schema for v on this client's registry.

methods.name: Registry
methods.doc: Registry returns the client's model registry.

methods.name: Transaction
methods.signature: func (c *Client) Transaction(ctx context.Context, fn func(tx *Tx) error) (err error)
methods.doc:
Transaction runs fn inside a database transaction. If fn returns an error, or
panics, the transaction is rolled back and the error (or a re-raised panic)
is propagated; otherwise it is committed. This is the interactive transaction
model: fn may issue any number of queries, inspect their results, and decide
what to do next.

	err := client.Transaction(ctx, func(tx *prisma.Tx) error {
	    from, err := prisma.NewTxQuery[Account](tx).
	        Where(prisma.Equals("id", 1)).FindUnique(ctx)
	    if err != nil {
	        return err
	    }
	    _, err = prisma.NewTxQuery[Account](tx).
	        Where(prisma.Equals("id", 1)).
	        Update(ctx, map[string]any{"balance": from.Balance - 100})
	    return err
	})

types.name: Condition
types.doc:
Condition is a boolean predicate used in WHERE clauses. Conditions compile
to parameterized SQL fragments; literal values are always bound as arguments,
never concatenated into the SQL text.

Construct conditions with the operator helpers (Equals, Not, In, Lt, Gt,
Contains, ...) and combine them with And, Or and NotGroup.

funcs.name: And
funcs.doc: And combines conditions with logical AND.

funcs.name: Between
funcs.signature: func Between(column string, low, high any) Condition
funcs.doc:
Between matches rows where column is within the inclusive range [low, high].
It is shorthand for combining [Gte] and [Lte] and compiles to a single SQL
BETWEEN term with both bounds bound as arguments.

funcs.name: Contains
funcs.doc: Contains matches rows where column contains the substring (LIKE %s%).

funcs.name: EndsWith
funcs.doc: EndsWith matches rows where column ends with the suffix (LIKE %s).

funcs.name: Equals
funcs.signature: func Equals(column string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc: Equals matches rows where column = value.

funcs.name: Every
funcs.doc:
Every matches base rows for which every related row satisfies conds
(vacuously true when there are no related rows), mirroring Prisma's "every".

funcs.name: Gt
funcs.signature: func Gt(column string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc: Gt matches rows where column > value.

funcs.name: Gte
funcs.signature: func Gte(column string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc: Gte matches rows where column >= value.

funcs.name: IContains
funcs.doc:
IContains is the case-insensitive form of [Contains], mirroring Prisma's
`contains` with `mode: "insensitive"`. Matching is performed by lowercasing
both the column (via SQL LOWER) and the bound pattern (via Go
strings.ToLower), so it is reliable for ASCII text on any engine.

funcs.name: IEndsWith
funcs.doc:
IEndsWith is the case-insensitive form of [EndsWith] (Prisma's `endsWith`
with `mode: "insensitive"`).

funcs.name: IEquals
funcs.doc:
IEquals is the case-insensitive form of [Equals] for string columns,
mirroring Prisma's `equals` with `mode: "insensitive"`. Both sides are
lowercased (SQL LOWER and Go strings.ToLower) before comparison.

funcs.name: IStartsWith
funcs.doc:
IStartsWith is the case-insensitive form of [StartsWith] (Prisma's
`startsWith` with `mode: "insensitive"`).

funcs.name: In
funcs.doc: In matches rows where column is any of values.

funcs.name: Is
funcs.doc:
Is matches base rows whose single related row (of a to-one relation)
satisfies conds, mirroring Prisma's "is".

funcs.name: IsNotNull
funcs.doc:
IsNotNull matches rows where column IS NOT NULL, mirroring Prisma's
`not: null` scalar filter.

funcs.name: IsNull
funcs.doc:
IsNull matches rows where column IS NULL. This mirrors Prisma's `equals: null`
scalar filter and cannot be expressed with [Equals] (SQL "= NULL" never
matches).

funcs.name: JSONContains
funcs.signature: func JSONContains(column string, path []string, substr string) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONContains matches rows where the extracted JSON string value contains
substr, mirroring Prisma's `string_contains` JSON filter. Wildcards in substr
are escaped.

funcs.name: JSONEndsWith
funcs.signature: func JSONEndsWith(column string, path []string, suffix string) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONEndsWith matches rows where the extracted JSON string value ends with
suffix, mirroring Prisma's `string_ends_with` JSON filter.

funcs.name: JSONEquals
funcs.signature: func JSONEquals(column string, path []string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONEquals matches rows where the value at path within the JSON column equals
value. It is shorthand for [JSONPath] with the "=" operator.

funcs.name: JSONGt
funcs.signature: func JSONGt(column string, path []string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONGt matches rows where the extracted JSON value is greater than value,
mirroring Prisma's `gt` JSON filter.

funcs.name: JSONGte
funcs.signature: func JSONGte(column string, path []string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONGte matches rows where the extracted JSON value is greater than or equal
to value, mirroring Prisma's `gte` JSON filter.

funcs.name: JSONIsNotNull
funcs.doc:
JSONIsNotNull matches rows where the value at path within the JSON column is
present and not null.

funcs.name: JSONIsNull
funcs.doc:
JSONIsNull matches rows where the value at path within the JSON column is
absent or JSON null (the extraction yields SQL NULL).

funcs.name: JSONLt
funcs.signature: func JSONLt(column string, path []string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONLt matches rows where the extracted JSON value is less than value,
mirroring Prisma's `lt` JSON filter.

funcs.name: JSONLte
funcs.signature: func JSONLte(column string, path []string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONLte matches rows where the extracted JSON value is less than or equal to
value, mirroring Prisma's `lte` JSON filter.

funcs.name: JSONNotContains
funcs.signature: func JSONNotContains(column string, path []string, substr string) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONNotContains matches rows where the extracted JSON string value does not
contain substr, mirroring Prisma's `not: { string_contains }` JSON filter.

funcs.name: JSONNotEquals
funcs.signature: func JSONNotEquals(column string, path []string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONNotEquals matches rows where the value at path within the JSON column does
not equal value. It is shorthand for [JSONPath] with the "<>" operator and
mirrors Prisma's `not` JSON filter.

funcs.name: JSONPath
funcs.signature: func JSONPath(column string, path []string, op string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONPath matches rows where the value at path within the JSON column compares
to value using op (e.g. "=", "<>", "<", ">"). The extraction syntax adapts to
the dialect (json_extract for SQLite/MySQL, #>> for PostgreSQL).

	prisma.JSONPath("data", []string{"address", "city"}, "=", "Paris")

funcs.name: JSONStartsWith
funcs.signature: func JSONStartsWith(column string, path []string, prefix string) Condition
funcs.doc:
JSONStartsWith matches rows where the extracted JSON string value begins with
prefix, mirroring Prisma's `string_starts_with` JSON filter.

funcs.name: Lt
funcs.signature: func Lt(column string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc: Lt matches rows where column < value.

funcs.name: Lte
funcs.signature: func Lte(column string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc: Lte matches rows where column <= value.

funcs.name: None
funcs.doc:
None matches base rows for which no related row satisfies conds (or that have
no related rows at all), mirroring Prisma's "none".

funcs.name: Not
funcs.signature: func Not(column string, value any) Condition
funcs.doc: Not matches rows where column <> value.

funcs.name: NotBetween
funcs.signature: func NotBetween(column string, low, high any) Condition
funcs.doc:
NotBetween matches rows where column falls outside the inclusive range
[low, high], compiling to a SQL NOT BETWEEN term.

funcs.name: NotContains
funcs.doc:
NotContains matches rows where column does not contain the substring,
mirroring Prisma's `not: { contains }`. Wildcard characters in substr are
escaped so only literal matching is performed.

funcs.name: NotEndsWith
funcs.doc:
NotEndsWith matches rows where column does not end with suffix, mirroring
Prisma's `not: { endsWith }`.

funcs.name: NotGroup
funcs.doc: NotGroup negates a (possibly compound) condition: NOT ( ... ).

funcs.name: NotIn
funcs.doc: NotIn matches rows where column is none of values.

funcs.name: NotStartsWith
funcs.doc:
NotStartsWith matches rows where column does not begin with prefix, mirroring
Prisma's `not: { startsWith }`.

funcs.name: Or
funcs.doc: Or combines conditions with logical OR.

funcs.name: RawCondition
funcs.doc:
RawCondition is an escape hatch for predicates the typed operators cannot
express. The expr is an arbitrary boolean SQL fragment using "?" placeholders;
each "?" is re-rendered in the client's [Dialect] placeholder style and the
corresponding arg is bound safely (never concatenated). The number of "?"
markers must equal len(args).

	prisma.RawCondition("length(name) > ?", 3)

funcs.name: Some
funcs.doc:
Some matches base rows for which at least one related row (of the named
to-many relation) satisfies conds. With no conditions it matches base rows
that have any related row.

funcs.name: StartsWith
funcs.doc: StartsWith matches rows where column begins with the prefix (LIKE s%).

types.name: Dialect
types.doc:
Dialect controls SQL syntax that varies between database engines: placeholder
rendering, DISTINCT ON support, NULLS ordering, JSON extraction and upsert
syntax. The three built-in dialects ([Question], [Dollar], [MySQLDialect])
cover SQLite, PostgreSQL and MySQL respectively.

types.name: Direction
types.signature: type Direction string
types.doc: Direction is a sort order for OrderBy.

types.name: Error
types.doc:
Error is a typed database error with a Prisma-style [Error.Code]. Execution
methods wrap driver errors in an *Error so callers can branch on the code
(via [errors.As]) or match a sentinel (via [errors.Is]) instead of parsing
vendor-specific message strings.

methods.name: Error
methods.doc: Error implements the error interface.

methods.name: Is
methods.doc:
Is reports whether target is an *Error with the same Code, which lets the
package's sentinel errors ([ErrUniqueViolation] and friends) match any
wrapped error carrying that code.

methods.name: Unwrap
methods.doc:
Unwrap returns the underlying driver error so [errors.Is] and [errors.As] can
traverse to it.

types.name: Field
types.doc:
Field describes a single mapped struct field. A Field is either a column
(Relation == nil) or a relation to another model (Relation != nil).

types.name: GroupQuery
types.doc:
GroupQuery builds a GROUP BY aggregation over the rows selected by a base
[Query]. It selects the grouping columns plus any requested aggregates, and
supports a HAVING filter over the aggregated rows.

methods.name: Avg
methods.doc: Avg adds AVG(column) to each group for every column.

methods.name: Count
methods.doc: Count adds COUNT(*) to each group.

methods.name: Exec
methods.doc:
Exec runs the grouped aggregation and returns one row per group. Each row maps
column/alias names to their scanned values (grouping keys and aggregates).

methods.name: Having
methods.doc:
Having adds a filter applied after grouping. Conditions typically reference
an aggregate alias (e.g. prisma.Gt("_count", 1)) or a raw aggregate
expression.

methods.name: Max
methods.doc: Max adds MAX(column) to each group for every column.

methods.name: Min
methods.doc: Min adds MIN(column) to each group for every column.

methods.name: SQL
methods.doc:
SQL returns the GROUP BY SELECT statement and bound arguments without
executing it.

methods.name: Sum
methods.doc: Sum adds SUM(column) to each group for every column.

types.name: Model
types.doc:
Model is the compiled schema for a Go struct: its table, columns, primary
key and relations. Models are produced by a Registry.

methods.name: Columns
methods.doc: Columns returns the SQL column names in declaration order.

methods.name: FieldByColumn
methods.doc: FieldByColumn returns the column field with the given SQL name.

methods.name: RelationByName
methods.doc: Relation returns the relation field with the given Go field name.

types.name: NestedCreate
types.doc:
NestedCreate builds a parent INSERT together with dependent writes on its
to-many relations (create / connect / connectOrCreate / nested update),
executed as one transaction. The parent's generated primary key is propagated
into each child statement's foreign key.

	res, err := prisma.NewQuery[User](client).
	    NestedCreate(User{Name: "Ada"}).
	    Create("Posts", Post{Title: "Hello"}).
	    Connect("Posts", prisma.Equals("id", 7)).
	    Exec(ctx)

methods.name: Connect
methods.doc:
Connect schedules linking existing related rows matching where to the new
parent (an UPDATE of their foreign key).

methods.name: ConnectOrCreate
methods.signature: func (n *NestedCreate[T]) ConnectOrCreate(relation string, where []Condition, child any) *NestedCreate[T]
methods.doc:
ConnectOrCreate links the related row matching where if it exists, otherwise
inserts child. The choice is made at execution time via a existence check.

methods.name: Create
methods.doc:
Create schedules inserting each child into the named relation, with its
foreign key set to the new parent's primary key.

methods.name: Exec
methods.doc:
Exec runs the nested write in a transaction: the parent is inserted first,
then each dependent operation runs with the parent's generated primary key.
The returned value is the parent with its auto primary key populated.

methods.name: Statements
methods.doc:
Statements returns the parent INSERT followed by the deterministic child
statements, using parentID as the linking foreign-key value. A
connectOrCreate operation is rendered as its create (INSERT) branch; at
execution time [NestedCreate.Exec] chooses connect or create per row.

methods.name: Update
methods.signature: func (n *NestedCreate[T]) Update(relation string, where []Condition, data map[string]any) *NestedCreate[T]
methods.doc:
Update schedules updating related rows matching where (scoped to the new
parent) with data.

types.name: Nulls
types.signature: type Nulls int
types.doc:
Nulls controls where NULL values sort relative to non-NULL values in an
ORDER BY term.

types.name: Option
types.signature: type Option func(*Client)
types.doc: Option configures a Client.

funcs.name: WithDialect
funcs.doc: WithDialect sets the SQL dialect (default: Question).

funcs.name: WithRegistry
funcs.doc: WithRegistry uses an existing registry instead of a fresh one.

types.name: Query
types.doc:
Query is a fluent, type-safe builder for a single model T. Methods return the
receiver so calls can be chained. Nothing touches the database until a
terminal method (FindMany, Create, Count, ...) is called; the *SQL variants
return the generated SQL and arguments without executing.

funcs.name: NewQuery
funcs.doc:
NewQuery starts a new query for the model T. If T is not yet registered on
the client it is registered lazily; any registration error is deferred and
surfaced by the terminal method.

funcs.name: NewTxQuery
funcs.doc:
NewTxQuery starts a new query for model T that executes against the
transaction tx instead of the client's connection pool. It is otherwise
identical to [NewQuery].

methods.name: Aggregate
methods.doc: Aggregate begins an aggregation over the query's WHERE predicate.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc:
Clone returns an independent copy of the query whose slice-valued state
(conditions, ordering, selection, includes, distinct columns) can be extended
without affecting the original. It lets a partially-built query serve as a
reusable base for several derived queries.

methods.name: Count
methods.doc: Count executes SELECT COUNT(*) and returns the number of matching rows.

methods.name: CountDistinct
methods.signature: func (q *Query[T]) CountDistinct(ctx context.Context, column string) (int64, error)
methods.doc:
CountDistinct executes [Query.CountDistinctSQL] and returns the number of
distinct non-NULL values of column among the matching rows, mirroring
Prisma's `_count` on a distinct field.

methods.name: CountDistinctSQL
methods.doc:
CountDistinctSQL returns a "SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT column) FROM table WHERE ..."
statement and its bound arguments without executing it. The column must be a
mapped column of the model.

methods.name: CountSQL
methods.doc: CountSQL returns the COUNT(*) statement and bound arguments.

methods.name: Create
methods.signature: func (q *Query[T]) Create(ctx context.Context, value T) (T, error)
methods.doc:
Create inserts a single row. If the model's primary key is auto-generated and
integer-typed, the generated id is written back into the returned value.

methods.name: CreateMany
methods.doc:
CreateMany inserts multiple rows in one statement and returns the number of
rows affected.

methods.name: CreateManySQL
methods.doc: CreateManySQL returns a multi-row INSERT statement and bound arguments.

methods.name: CreateSQL
methods.doc: CreateSQL returns the single-row INSERT statement and bound arguments.

methods.name: Cursor
methods.doc:
Cursor sets a keyset pagination anchor: only rows strictly after value in the
sort direction of column are returned. The column should also appear in an
[Query.OrderBy] term; its direction determines whether the anchor uses ">"
(Asc) or "<" (Desc). Combine with [Query.Skip] and [Query.Take] for
forward pagination.

methods.name: Delete
methods.doc: Delete removes every matching row and returns the number of rows affected.

methods.name: DeleteMany
methods.doc:
DeleteMany is an alias for [Query.Delete] using Prisma's method name: it
removes every matching row and returns the number affected.

methods.name: DeleteSQL
methods.doc: DeleteSQL returns the DELETE statement and bound arguments.

methods.name: Distinct
methods.doc:
Distinct restricts the result to distinct rows. With no columns it emits a
plain SELECT DISTINCT; with columns on a dialect that supports it (see
[Dialect.DistinctOn]) it emits SELECT DISTINCT ON (columns), otherwise it
falls back to SELECT DISTINCT.

methods.name: Exists
methods.doc:
Exists reports whether at least one row matches the query's WHERE predicate,
evaluating the [Query.ExistsSQL] statement. It is a convenience over counting
when only presence matters.

methods.name: ExistsSQL
methods.doc:
ExistsSQL returns a statement that evaluates to a single boolean-ish row
reporting whether any row matches the query's WHERE predicate, of the form
"SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM table WHERE ...)". It does not touch the
database.

methods.name: FindFirst
methods.doc:
FindFirst executes the SELECT with LIMIT 1 and returns the first matching
row, or ErrNotFound.

methods.name: FindFirstOrThrow
methods.doc:
FindFirstOrThrow executes the query like [Query.FindFirst] but returns the
typed [ErrRecordNotFound] (Prisma code P2025) instead of a plain sentinel when
no row matches, mirroring Prisma's findFirstOrThrow. Any other error is
returned unchanged.

methods.name: FindMany
methods.doc: FindMany executes the SELECT and scans every row into a slice of T.

methods.name: FindManySQL
methods.doc: FindManySQL returns the SELECT statement and bound arguments for FindMany.

methods.name: FindUnique
methods.doc:
FindUnique is an alias for FindFirst intended for lookups by a unique column;
callers supply the uniqueness via Where.

methods.name: FindUniqueOrThrow
methods.doc:
FindUniqueOrThrow executes the query like [Query.FindUnique] but returns the
typed [ErrRecordNotFound] (Prisma code P2025) when no row matches, mirroring
Prisma's findUniqueOrThrow.

methods.name: GroupBy
methods.doc: GroupBy begins a grouped aggregation keyed by the given columns.

methods.name: Include
methods.doc:
Include eagerly loads a to-one relation by its Go field name using a LEFT
JOIN, selecting the related model's columns alongside the base row.

methods.name: NestedCreate
methods.doc: NestedCreate starts a nested write whose root inserts value.

methods.name: OrWhere
methods.doc:
OrWhere adds a single predicate that is the logical OR of conds, combined with
the rest of the query's predicate using AND. It is shorthand for
Where([Or](conds...)) and mirrors Prisma's top-level `OR` array.

	q.Where(prisma.Equals("active", true)).
	  OrWhere(prisma.Equals("role", "admin"), prisma.Equals("role", "owner"))
	// ... WHERE active = ? AND (role = ? OR role = ?)

methods.name: OrderBy
methods.doc:
OrderBy adds an ORDER BY term. Multiple calls accumulate, producing a
multi-column ordering in call order.

methods.name: OrderByNulls
methods.signature: func (q *Query[T]) OrderByNulls(column string, dir Direction, nulls Nulls) *Query[T]
methods.doc:
OrderByNulls adds an ORDER BY term with explicit NULL placement. On engines
without native NULLS syntax (see [Dialect.NativeNulls]) the placement is
emulated with a leading "column IS NULL" sort term.

methods.name: Paginate
methods.doc:
Paginate sets LIMIT/OFFSET for 1-based offset pagination: page 1 returns the
first size rows, page 2 the next size, and so on. It is a convenience over
setting [Query.Take] and [Query.Skip] by hand. page and size are clamped to a
minimum of 1 and 0 respectively.

methods.name: Select
methods.doc:
Select restricts the projection to the named columns. Without it, all mapped
columns are selected.

methods.name: Skip
methods.doc: Skip offsets the returned rows (SQL OFFSET).

methods.name: SkipDuplicates
methods.doc:
SkipDuplicates makes the next [Query.CreateMany] ignore rows that violate a
unique constraint instead of failing (ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING / INSERT IGNORE,
per the dialect's [Dialect.Upsert] style).

methods.name: Take
methods.doc: Take limits the number of rows returned (SQL LIMIT).

methods.name: Update
methods.signature: func (q *Query[T]) Update(ctx context.Context, data map[string]any) (int64, error)
methods.doc:
Update applies the column assignments to every matching row and returns the
number of rows affected.

methods.name: UpdateMany
methods.signature: func (q *Query[T]) UpdateMany(ctx context.Context, data map[string]any) (int64, error)
methods.doc:
UpdateMany is an alias for [Query.Update] using Prisma's method name: it
applies data to every matching row and returns the number affected.

methods.name: UpdateSQL
methods.doc:
UpdateSQL returns the UPDATE statement and bound arguments for the given
column assignments. Column keys are sorted so the output is deterministic.

methods.name: Upsert
methods.signature: func (q *Query[T]) Upsert(ctx context.Context, conflictColumns []string, create T, update map[string]any) (int64, error)
methods.doc:
Upsert executes an insert-or-update (see [Query.UpsertSQL]) and returns the
number of rows affected.

methods.name: UpsertSQL
methods.signature: func (q *Query[T]) UpsertSQL(conflictColumns []string, create T, update map[string]any) (string, []any, error)
methods.doc:
UpsertSQL returns an "insert-or-update" statement and its bound arguments. If
a row conflicting on conflictColumns already exists, the update assignments
are applied; otherwise create is inserted. The syntax follows the dialect's
[Dialect.Upsert] style: ON CONFLICT (PostgreSQL/SQLite) or ON DUPLICATE KEY
UPDATE (MySQL). Update column keys are sorted for deterministic output.

methods.name: Where
methods.doc: Where appends conditions, combined with the existing predicate using AND.

methods.name: WhereRaw
methods.doc:
WhereRaw appends a raw SQL boolean fragment to the query's predicate,
combined with any existing conditions using AND. The expr uses "?"
placeholders that are re-rendered in the client's [Dialect] and whose args are
bound safely; it is the [Query]-level shorthand for
Where([RawCondition](expr, args...)).

types.name: Registry
types.doc:
Registry stores compiled models and lets them be resolved by type or name.
The zero value is not usable; construct one with NewRegistry.

funcs.name: NewRegistry
funcs.doc: NewRegistry returns an empty registry.

methods.name: ModelOf
methods.doc: ModelOf returns the compiled model for the given type, if registered.

methods.name: Register
methods.doc:
Register compiles the schema for v (a struct or pointer to struct) via
reflection and struct tags, stores it, and returns the model. Re-registering
the same type returns the previously compiled model.

Struct tags use the key "prisma" with comma-separated options:

	col=<name>    explicit column name (default: snake_case of the field)
	pk            marks the primary key
	auto          value is generated by the DB (skipped on INSERT)
	-             skip the field entirely
	relation      the field links to another model
	fk=<col>      relation: foreign-key column on this model
	references=<col> relation: referenced column on the target (default: its PK)

A field whose type is a pointer-to-struct or slice-of-struct and is tagged
"relation" becomes a relation rather than a column.

types.name: Relation
types.doc: Relation describes a to-one or to-many link between two models.

types.name: Statement
types.doc:
Statement is a single generated SQL statement with its bound arguments, as
produced by a nested write's planning method.

types.name: TableNamer
types.doc:
TableNamer lets a model type override the table name derived from its Go
type name. Implement it on the struct (value or pointer receiver) to control
the exact table used in generated SQL.

types.name: Tx
types.doc:
Tx is a handle to an in-progress database transaction. Start queries against
it with [NewTxQuery]; every statement they run participates in the same
transaction. A Tx is created by [Client.Transaction] (interactive) or
[Client.Batch] (batched) and must not be used after the enclosing call
returns.

methods.name: Client
methods.doc: Client returns the owning [Client].

methods.name: ExecuteRaw
methods.signature: func (t *Tx) ExecuteRaw(ctx context.Context, query string, args ...any) (int64, error)
methods.doc:
ExecuteRaw runs a raw statement within the transaction; see
[Client.ExecuteRaw].

methods.name: QueryRaw
methods.signature: func (t *Tx) QueryRaw(ctx context.Context, query string, args ...any) ([]map[string]any, error)
methods.doc: QueryRaw runs a raw query within the transaction; see [Client.QueryRaw].

methods.name: SQLTx
methods.doc:
SQLTx returns the underlying *sql.Tx for escape-hatch use (e.g. raw
statements not expressible through the builder).

types.name: UpdateOp
types.doc:
UpdateOp is an atomic column update expression used as a value in the map
passed to [Query.Update] / [Query.UpdateSQL]. Instead of assigning a literal,
it computes the new value from the column's current value, mirroring Prisma's
numeric atomic operations ({ increment }, { decrement }, { multiply },
{ divide }, { set }). The generated SQL is always parameterized, e.g.
"balance = balance + ?".

funcs.name: Decrement
funcs.doc:
Decrement returns an [UpdateOp] that subtracts delta from the column's current
value ("col = col - ?"), mirroring Prisma's `{ decrement: delta }`.

funcs.name: Divide
funcs.doc:
Divide returns an [UpdateOp] that divides the column's current value by
divisor ("col = col / ?"), mirroring Prisma's `{ divide: divisor }`.

funcs.name: Increment
funcs.doc:
Increment returns an [UpdateOp] that adds delta to the column's current value
("col = col + ?"), mirroring Prisma's `{ increment: delta }`.

funcs.name: Multiply
funcs.doc:
Multiply returns an [UpdateOp] that multiplies the column's current value by
factor ("col = col * ?"), mirroring Prisma's `{ multiply: factor }`.

funcs.name: SetTo
funcs.doc:
SetTo returns an [UpdateOp] that assigns value directly ("col = ?"). It is the
explicit form of Prisma's `{ set: value }` and is equivalent to placing value
in the update map unwrapped; it exists so a set can sit alongside other
[UpdateOp] values uniformly.

types.name: UpsertStyle
types.signature: type UpsertStyle int
types.doc:
UpsertStyle selects the SQL syntax a dialect uses for upserts and
skip-duplicate inserts. The two dominant families are supported.

funcs.name: QueryRawInto
funcs.signature: func QueryRawInto[T any](ctx context.Context, c *Client, query string, args ...any) ([]T, error)
funcs.doc:
QueryRawInto runs a raw query and scans each row into a value of type T,
matching result columns to T's mapped columns by name (unmatched columns are
ignored, missing ones left zero). T must be registered on the client, or
registrable. This is the typed form of [Client.QueryRaw].

examples.code:
client := NewClient(nil)
if _, err := client.Register(User{}); err != nil {
	panic(err)
}

q := NewQuery[User](client).
	Where(Equals("email", "a@b.com"), Gt("id", 10)).
	OrderBy("name", Asc).
	Take(5)

sqlStr, args, err := q.FindManySQL()
if err != nil {
	panic(err)
}
fmt.Println(sqlStr)
fmt.Println(args...)
examples.output:
SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE email = ? AND id > ? ORDER BY name ASC LIMIT ?
a@b.com 10 5
examples.doc:
Example builds a query and prints the parameterized SQL and arguments it
generates, without touching a database.

examples.name: Some
examples.output:
SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM posts WHERE posts.author_id = users.id AND title = ?)
Hello
examples.doc:
ExampleSome shows a relation filter compiling to a correlated EXISTS
subquery: users who have at least one published post.

examples.name: Query (Aggregate)
examples.output:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS _count, SUM(amount) AS _sum_amount, AVG(amount) AS _avg_amount FROM orders WHERE status = ?
paid
examples.doc: ExampleQuery_Aggregate shows building an aggregation over a filtered set.

examples.name: Query (UpsertSQL)
examples.output:
INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?) ON CONFLICT (email) DO UPDATE SET name = ?
Ada ada@example.com Ada Lovelace
